coredata.dc:title: Preparation and performance of a new silylated functional carbon quantum dots corrosion inhibitor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Mi
He, Ping
Zhang, Hongjiang
Dong, Sheying
Huang, Tinglin
coredata.dc:description: Adding green corrosion inhibitors to prevent the pickling process is critical to both corrosion of carbon steel and its environmental protection. Carbon quantum dots(CDs) corrosion inhibitors prepared using a hydrothermal method are considered promising candidates for carbon steel corrosion protection. Here, a new high-efficiency silylated functional carbon quantum dot (CDCA-Si-Pei) was prepared by hydrothermal method and evaluated for its corrosion inhibition performance as a corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in hydrochloric acid solution (1 M HCl). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis revealed that the surface roughness of carbon steel treated with CDCA-Si-Pei was significantly reduced, the surface was free of corrosion products, and the adsorption mechanism of CDCA-Si-Pei was chemical and physical co-adsorption. The experimental results show that CDCA-Si-Pei is a highly efficient and stable inhibitor with obvious anti-corrosion effects, especially at a concentration of 10 mg/L and its corrosion inhibition rate of 96.34 % and 94.79 % after three months of placement.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green inhibitor
High-efficiency
Hydrochloric acid solution
Adsorption mechanism
Carbon steel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FTIR, UV–vis and fluorescence spectra analysis of CDCA-Si-Pei, CDCA-Si, CDCA-Pei
Corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation schematic of CDCA-Si-Pei.
FTIR, UV–vis and Fluorescence spectra of as-prepared CDCA-Si-Pei; CDCA-Si; CDCA-Pei (a) FTIR; (b) UV–vis; (c) Fluorescence emission spectra; (d) Excitation and emission spectra-CDCA-Si-Pei.
TEM image and grain size of CDCA-Si-Pei.
Tafel curve of all carbon steel specimens in various test solutions.
EIS all carbon steel specimens in various test solutions.
Equivalent circuit.
Relationship between inhibition efficiency/corrosion rate and inhibitor concentration as well as immersion time (a) immersion time；(b) inhibitor concentration.
Surface morphology of Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 mol/L HCl solution (a)Black; (b) soaked for 24 h; (c) soaked for 144 h.

Element distribution of carbon steel in various immersion time (a) blank; (b) blank-O; (c) blank-Cl; (d) graph of element content-blank;(e) 24 h; (f) 24 h-O; (g) 24 h-Cl; (h) graph of element content-24 h; (i) 144 h; (j) 144 h-O; (k) 144 h-Cl; (l) graph of element content-144 h.
3D AFM morphology of carbon steel in immersion time (a) blank; (b)24 h; (c)144 h.
(a)Langmuir adsorption isotherms of as-obtained; (b)Corrosion products of Raman analysis after immersion.
(a) Corrosion mechanism of Q235 carbon steel; (b) corrosion mechanism of CDCA-Si-Pei; (c) the adsorption mechanism of carbon steel and CDCA-Si-Pei.
Polarization curve parameters of carbon steel in various CDs inhibitors.
Polarization curve parameters of carbon steel in diverse test temperature of in CDCA-Si-Pei.
Polarization curve parameters of carbon steel in diverse immersion times of in CDCA-Si-Pei.
Polarization curve parameters of carbon steel in CDCA-Si-Pei which after 3 months.
Impedance parameters of carbon steel in various CDs inhibitors.
Impedance parameters of carbon steel in diverse test temperature of in CDCA-Si-Pei.
Impedance parameters of carbon steel in diverse immersion times of in CDCA-Si-Pei.
Impedance parameters of carbon steel in CDCA-Si-Pei which after 3 M.
Comparison of η (%) and concentration of corrosion inhibitor between the results of this work and others inhibitors in the literatures.

head.ce:title: Preparation and performance of a new silylated functional carbon quantum dots corrosion inhibitor

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi′an University of Architecture and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Adding green corrosion inhibitors to prevent the pickling process is critical to both corrosion of carbon steel and its environmental protection. Carbon quantum dots(CDs) corrosion inhibitors prepared using a hydrothermal method are considered promising candidates for carbon steel corrosion protection. Here, a new high-efficiency silylated functional carbon quantum dot (CDCA-Si-Pei) was prepared by hydrothermal method and evaluated for its corrosion inhibition performance as a corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in hydrochloric acid solution (1 M HCl). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis revealed that the surface roughness of carbon steel treated with CDCA-Si-Pei was significantly reduced, the surface was free of corrosion products, and the adsorption mechanism of CDCA-Si-Pei was chemical and physical co-adsorption. The experimental results show that CDCA-Si-Pei is a highly efficient and stable inhibitor with obvious anti-corrosion effects, especially at a concentration of 10 mg/L and its corrosion inhibition rate of 96.34 % and 94.79 % after three months of placement.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request. No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon steel is a low-cost material with good mechanical properties. As such, it is widely used as a construction material in various industries. The corrosion phenomenon is a spontaneous process for carbon steel devices in the natural environment, which usually damages industrial production [1]. Most carbon steel corrosion mainly occurs in the ocean, oil pipelines, marine steel pile bridges and other water systems. The most common carbon steel corrosion is salt and acid corrosion in seawater, etc [2]. Strong acid can wash away the carbon steel equipment or pipeline surface dirt, corrosion oxide film and rust corrosion material. However, as the pickling solution is a strong acid (such as hydrochloric acid, sulphuric acid, nitric acid and phosphoric acid, etc [3].) and is generally highly corrosive, it can also cause corrosion while removing rust and dirt from the surface of carbon steel. Accordingly, intensive effects are being carried out to prevent metal corrosion [4], such as new corrosion-resistant alloys; [5] electrochemical protection (cathodic and anodic protection);[6,7] the addition of corrosion inhibitors;[8,9] and coatings [10,11]. Common corrosion inhibitors are chromates, phosphates, and other inorganic salts and heteroatoms containing N, P, O, S and other heterocyclic compounds [12,13]. These corrosion inhibitors are effective but expensive and not conducive to environmental friendliness. Developing green, non-toxic, efficient and environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors has become one of the hot spots of corrosion inhibitor research.

CDs are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials that have attracted the attention of many scholars due to their excellent biocompatibility and photochemical properties [14–16]. So far, the application of carbon dots has been discovered in the corrosion protective field and presented an excellent result. Such as Ren et al [17]. investigated the corrosion resistance of the pure epoxy coating and carbon dot/epoxy coating in 3.5 % NaCl solution, and it was found that the corrosion resistance improved significantly when immersed for 33 days; Ye et al. [18]. prepared a corrosion inhibitor for CDs modified imidazole using citric acid and N-(3-aminopropyl) as raw materials and found that when the dosage was 200 mg/L, the N-CDs corrosion inhibitor with a corrosion inhibition efficiency of 93.4 %; Yang et al. [19]. successfully prepared functionalised carbon dots of citric acid and discussed their corrosion inhibition properties in HCl and NaCl, after analysis, the functionalised carbon dots could adsorb on the steel surface and then inhibit the erosion of Cl-; Cen et al. [20]. synthesised a novel N, S-CD, and the study showed that when added N, S-CDs significantly improved the corrosion of carbon steel by CO2 and saturated NaCl solutions. Organosilane functionalised carbon quantum dots (SiCDs) are an essential strategy for modulating the physicochemical properties of CDs [21]. This is due to organosilane organic and alkoxy groups' ability to chemically react with the reactive functional groups on the surface of CDs under certain conditions, forming amide bonds, silicone bonds. modifying organosilanes onto the surface of CDs.

Zheng et al. [22]. used microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment of aqueous solutions of citric acid and KH-792 to obtain SiCDs solutions, and the solids of SiCDs exhibited bright solid-state fluorescence. He et al. [23]. used N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropylmethyldimethoxysilane (KH-602) and citric acid as raw materials and acetone as a solvent to synthesize organosilane-functionalised CDs, which fluoresced red in the solid state and blue in aqueous solution. The reaction time of SiCDs is shorter than that of conventional CDs [24–26], and they have a longer emission wavelength in the solid state.

In this study, a novel and efficient CDCA-Si-Pei was synthesized by a hydrothermal method using cellulose acetate(CA), polyethyleneimine(PEI) and N-aminoethyl-γ-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (DAMO), which exhibited relatively high inhibition efficiency and low cost compared to previous studies. The structure and composition of the prepared CDCA-Si-Pei were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR); ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV); fluorescence spectroscopy (FL), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The inhibition of the prepared CDCA-Si-Pei was evaluated by potentiodynamic polarization (Tafel) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). After corrosion, the corrosion inhibition mechanism was investigated by SEM and AFM analysis of carbon steel's 2D and 3D morphology.

Shanghai Titan Technology Co Ltd, McLean Corporation and Xi'an Chemical Reagent Factory supplied polyethyleneimine(PEI), N-aminoethyl-γ-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (DAMO) and cellulose acetate(CA). The carbon steel of 1 × 1 cm2 was chosen for the testing electrode, which was polished by the sandpapers of 400, 800 and 1200 grids and washed by ultrasonic equipment in solute ethyl alcohol for 20 min.

The preparation of CDCA-Si-Pei is shown in Fig. 1. In detail, 0.5 g of PEI was first taken in a 50 mL beaker, then 0.5 g of CA was weighed and poured into a beaker containing the weighed PEI, and finally 1000 μL of DAMO was added using a 1000 μL pipette, 30 mL of deionised water was added and magnetically stirred for 10 min to make it homogeneous. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction was filtered through a qualitative filter paper. The light yellow solution was filtered through an injection filter (pore size 0.22 µm), then dialyzed with deionized water (number average molecular weight 1000 g/mol) for six hours. The distilled water was replaced every two hours, and the solution was freeze-dried after decontamination to give CDCA-Si-Pei for later use. For comparison, cellulose acetate-organosilicon carbon quantum dots (CDCA-Si) and cellulose acetate-polyethyleneimine carbon quantum dots (CDCA-Pei) were also synthesised under the same conditions as CDCA-Si-Pei.

The test environments were 1 M HCl solution with different concentrations of CDCA-Si-Pei (0, 5, 10, 15, 20 mg/L). Before preparation, the 1 M HCl solution was diluted through concentrated hydrochloric acid(37 %). A precision electronic scale weighed the quality of CDCA-Si-Pei.

The structure of CDCA-Si-Pei was measured by Nicolet ISQ 7000 Nicolet FTIR， F97PRO Fluorescence spectrophotometer and UV–Vis. FEI-Tecnai G2 F20 TEM acquired morphology and distribution of CDCA-Si-Pei.

The CHI660E electrochemical workstation was used to measure open circuit potential (OCP), EIS and polarization curve methods to assess the corrosion inhibition performance of the prepared corrosion inhibitors. A three-electrode system was used for electrochemical testing. A platinum sheet, a saturated glycerol electrode and carbon steel were used as the auxiliary, reference and working electrodes. Before each measurement, the working electrode was immersed in the corrosion inhibitor solution for 30 min to achieve a stable open circuit potential. Only one surface (1.0 cm2) was exposed to the solution during testing, while the rest was covered with paraffin. The EIS and polarization curves were then tested separately. All electrochemical tests were carried out at 303 K.

The corrosion rate was obtained according to the NACE standard. All specimens were weighed thrice through a precision electronic scale in a constant temperature thermostat. After that, samples were immersed in various test solutions for a different time, then rinsed with deionized water, and re-weighed to acquire the average corrosion rate (
ν
corr
), The corrosion rates of all samples were calculated from equation [27,28] (1).
(1)
ν
corr
=
W
1
−
W
0
S * t
where W
0 和W
1 was the weight loss before and after immersion (g), S was the test area (cm2), and t was the immersion time (h).

After the immersion test, the carbon steel was cleaned with deionised water and dried with nitrogen. The carbon steel's corrosion morphology and surface element distribution were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Sigma 300, Germany). The surface roughness of the specimens was measured by atomic force microscopy (3D, Bruker Dimension Icon, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR, UV–vis and fluorescence spectra analysis of CDCA-Si-Pei, CDCA-Si, CDCA-Pei
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the FTIR, UV–vis and Fluorescence spectra of as-prepared CDCA-Si-Pei， CDCA-Si and CDCA-Pei. In the case of FTIR spectra (Fig. 2(a))， which can be seen that at 1123 cm−1, 1396 cm−1, 1502 cm−1, 1656 cm−1, 2840 cm−1, 3382 cm−1 correspond to the telescopic vibrational absorption peaks of C-N, N-H, CO, C-H at 1572 cm−1 in the infrared spectra of CDCA-Si and CDCA-Si-Pei, the existence of these functional groups was beneficial to attain excellent hydrophilicity of the synthesized carbon dots [29]. While an absorption peak at 783 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration peak of Si-O, while the stretching vibration peak of Si-O did not appear in the infrared absorption peak of CDCA, thus indicating that the CDCA-Si, CDCA-Pei and CDCA-Si-Pei were successfully synthesised, indicating successful doping with organosilanes, suggesting amination reactions between CA, PEI and DAMO. For UV–vis spectra(Fig. 2(b)), CDCA-Si showed a maximum absorption peak at a wavelength of 306 nm. CDCA-Si-Pei and CDCA-Si-Pei showed a maximum absorption peak at a wavelength of 359 nm, which indicated that CDCA-Pei and CDCA-Si-Pei are red-shifted compared to CDCA-Si, which was related to the n-π * electron leap in the CN bond [30], indicating DAMO was successfully doped. CDCA-Pei and CDCA-Si-Pei showed a maximum absorption peak at a wavelength of 209 nm, possibly due to the π→π * electron leap of sp2. Foer fluorescence spectra，the maximum emission wavelength (Fig. 2(c)) of the synthesized CDCA-Si, CDCA-Pei and CDCA-Si-Pei were 447 nm, 463 nm 424 nm, respectively. The successful preparation of CDCA-Si-Pei can be confirmed by comparing the maximum emission wavelengths of the three kinds of CDs. The excitation and emission wavelengths(Fig. 2(d)) of CDCA-Si-Pei are 373 nm and 424 nm, respectively, and the excitation and emission wavelengths were mirror-symmetric, which maked the emission wavelength larger than the excitation wavelength due to the existence of the stokes shift phenomenon.

The TEM image and grain size of CDCA-Si-Pei were shown in Fig. 3. From the particle size distribution plot on the right, the particle size analysis measurements calculated that the CDCA-Si-Pei particle size was mainly distributed in the range of 2.2–2.3 nm and 3.2–3.4 nm, and the overall CDCA-Si-Pei particle size was within 5 nm, the size of composite CDCA-Si-Pei nanometers, which also indicated the successful preparation of CDCA-Si-Pei [31], because the fluorescence property was a typical feature [32].

Fig. 4 Showed the Tafel data of all specimens in various test solutions. The polarization curves (a, b, c) after the addition of CDCA-Si, CDCA-Pei and CDCA-Si-Pei to the hydrochloric acid solution showed a shift towards lower current densities for both the anode and cathode compared to the current densities of the blank solution, indicating that both the Fe dissolution reaction at the anode and the hydrogen precipitation reaction at the cathode was inhibited. Among the three kinds of CDs inhibitors, the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei shifted the most, indicating that the adsorption of CDCA-Si-Pei on the active sites on the carbon steel surface effectively inhibited the corrosion of the carbon steel. the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei significantly prevented the further deposition of Fe atoms on the surface of the Q235 carbon steel at the anode and the discharge of H+ in the cathode region, increasing the superpotential of the hydrogen analysis reaction process and, therefore, the purpose of inhibition [33]. Meanwhile, this experiment investigated the stability of the CDCA-Si-Pei material itself, testing the effect of temperature and immersion time on its corrosion inhibition properties. As we can see by (d, e, f), the cathodic curve of the CDCA-Si-Pei polarization curve was almost always parallel to the cathodic curve of the blank solution as the temperature increased, the corrosion time grew, and the material was left for three months. Still, the shape and slope of the anodic curve changed significantly, indicating that the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei had a smaller effect on the reaction mechanism of the cathode, indicating that the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei had a negligible impact on the reaction mechanism of the cathode but a certain effect on the reaction mechanism of the anode.

Tafel curves and the results were listed in
Tables 1–4. The corrosion potential (E
corr ), corrosion current density (i
corr ), anodic Tafel slope (b
a ), cathodic Tafel slope (b
c ), degree of surface coverage (θ) and corrosion inhibition efficiency (IE) were the relevant parameter. Among them, the θ and IE could be calculated by Eq. (2) [34] and (3) [35].
(2)
θ =
i
0
corr
−
i
corr
i
0
corr
(3)
IE =
i
0
corr
−
i
corr
i
0
corr
× 100 %
where i
0
corr and i
corr represented the corrosion current densities without and with inhibitor solution, respectively. As can be seen from Table 1, when the concentration of the three kinds of CDs increased, the corrosion current density value was first decreased and then increased. At the same time, the trend of the change in the inhibition efficiency and the trend of the corrosion current density increased, the b
a and cathodic tafel slope b c both were changed, and the b c value changed more obviously, indicating that these CDs belonged to a mixed type of inhibitor to mainly inhibit cathodic corrosion, which the addition of these inhibitors significantly prevented the attack of carbon steel by hydrochloric acid [36]. The stability of the inhibitor was critical to its application; combined with Tables 2–4 can be observed that, with the increasing in test temperature and the raising of corrosion time and material placed after three months, corrosion current density were increased, inhibition efficiency although slightly decreased, the inhibition efficiency calculated by the Eq. (3) are 91.11 %, 90.95 % and 95.50 % respectively.

The EIS images of all steel and equivalent circuits in various test solutions were shown separately in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6. From Fig. 5(a,b,c), it can be clearly seen that by adding different concentrations of the three kinds of CDs inhibitors, the diameter of the impedance arc increased as the concentration of CDs increased, this was since CDs could form a valid film on the electrode surface, that was to say, the compactness of protective film was improved as the inhibitor concentration increased. The impedance arc diameter was the largest, and the inhibition efficiency was the highest. We observed the largest impedance arc radius with the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei, indicating that the corrosion inhibition performance of CDCA-Si-Pei was superior to that of CDCA-Si and CDCA-Pei. in Fig. 5(d, e, f), the impedance arc radius tended to decrease with increasing temperature, increasing corrosion time and after the material had been left for three months, and the inhibition rate changes in the same trend In addition, all of the Nyquist plots showed similar semicircles, indicating that the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei only increases the impedance arc diameter and enhances the corrosion resistance of the carbon steel, without changing other electrochemical properties in the corrosion regime.

All EIS data fitted via Zsimpwin were shown in
Tables 5–8, made up of R p, R
0 and CPE
dl, which were the polarization resistances without and with inhibitor solution and constant-phase angle element (CPE
dl ), respectively. During curve fitting, CPE
dl replaced ideal electric double-layer capacitance (C
dl ). In the circuit model, CPE
dl could more fully describe the actual situation than C
dl , which could be obtained through Eq. (4):[37].
(4)
Z
CPE
=
1
Y ( jw )
n

Thereinto, Y, w, j and n denoted the CPE magnitude, angular frequency, imaginary root and error parameter, respectively. An inductor (n = −1), resistor (n = 0), Warburg impedance (n = 0.5) and capacitor (n = 1) corresponded to different values of n. the CPE could represent an inductor, resistor, warburg impedance and capacitor, respectively. Meanwhile, the C
dl was calculated through Eq. (5):[38].
(5)
C =
Y ( 2 π f )
n − 1
where f was the frequency at the maximum value of the imaginary component in the Nyquist spectrum. The value of n used to reflect the surface roughness was inversely proportional to the roughness of the steel surface. As the increase of inhibitor concentration, the n value presented a downtrend. This was due to the inhibitor adsorbed on the surface of steel and then decreasing the surface homogeneity inhibition efficiency could be acquired through Eq.(6):
(6)
η =
R
p
−
R
0
R
p
× 100 %

Table 5 showed the impedance parameters of carbon steel in various CDs inhibitors. With increasing CDs concentration, R
p also increased, indicating that CDs corrosion inhibitors can adsorb well on the surface of carbon steel, forming adsorption films, thus inhibiting the transfer of electrons from the surface of carbon steel to the solution inhibiting the metal dissolution process. This was because CDs contain abundant lone pair electrons, which can be coordinated with the atomic iron vacancies on the carbon steel surface, thus occupying the empty orbitals of iron atoms for inhibition purposes [39]. The maximum inhibition efficiency was calculated from Eq. (6) to be 95.45 %, 95.75 % and 96.3 % for CDCA-Si, CDCA-Pei, and CDCA-Si-Pei at concentrations of 50 mg/L, 15 mg/L and 10 mg/L, respectively. This was consistent with the analysis of polarization parameters. Tables 6–8 revealed the effects of test temperature, immersion time and the stability of the material on its corrosion inhibition properties. Table 6 showed that as the test temperature increases, R
p decreases, and the inhibition efficiency decreases. This was probably because the activation energy of the reaction increases when the test temperature was too high. The stability of the CDCA-Si-Pei molecules became less stable, resulting in a decrease in the stability of the CDCA-Si-Pei protective film and, therefore, a partial detachment, thus aggravating the corrosion so that the corrosion inhibition performance decreased sharply at 323 K. However, overall, even at 323 K, the inhibition efficiency of CDCA-Si-Pei was still maintained at about 90 %, probably because the Si-O-Si group of the organosilicon effectively protected the functional groups on its surface, and also indicated that the thermal stability of CDCA-Si-Pei is relatively excellent.

Table 7 observed that the impedance decreased as the immersion time increases, with an inhibition efficiency of approximately 90 % at 144 h. On the one hand, this may be because the increase in time reduces the stability of the adsorption film. On the other hand, the carbon steel working electrode paraffin was soaked away, resulting in the shedding of the adsorption film. Table 8 showed that in the blank solution, the value of R
p was lower, probably due to the direct exposure of the carbon steel to the corrosive medium. However, after the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei, the R
p gradually increased, indicating that the adsorption of CDCA-Si-Pei on the surface of the carbon steel resulted in strong corrosion protection on the surface carbon steel, allowing the corrosion of the carbon steel to be mitigated. When the CDCA-Si-Pei (3 M) concentration was 10 mg/L, R
p reached the maximum. The inhibition efficiency was 94.8 %, the best corrosion inhibition effect consistented with the best concentration of CDCA-Si-Pei in the initial state, indicating the better stability of CDCA-Si-Pei itself.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The variations in corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency were displayed in Fig. 7. It can be seen that the
ν
corr
of carbon steel significantly reduced with the increase in immersion time (Fig. 7a) and the augmentation of CDCA-Si-Pei concentration (Fig. 7b). The trend of the inhibition efficiency was opposite to the corrosion rate. The inhibition efficiency was maintained at about 90 % even at 144 h immersion time and 96.34 % when the CDCA-Si-Pei concentration was 10 mg/L. Compared with other literature on CDs, the dosing concentration was generally at 200 mg/L, indicating that even at low concentrations, CDCA-Si-Pei formed a protective adsorption film on the surface of carbon steel for a long time, thus enabling it to have a high-efficiency inhibition efficiency.

The SEM corrosion morphologies of steel without CDCA-Si-Pei and the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei solution after immersion were obtained in Fig. 8. It can be seen that in the absence of the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei, the carbon steel The surface of the samples was severely corroded after immersion, with roughness and cracks and pits. At 24 h and 144 h of immersion, the surface of the carbon steel was very smooth, and the corrosion damage was significantly reduced, except for the marks from grinding. The results indicated that CDCA-Si-Pei formed a protective film on the surface of the carbon steel to effectively inhibit the penetration of aggressive particles into the carbon steel substrate, which was consistent with the results of the electrochemical test analysis. This phenomenon may be because the amide groups, oxygen heteroatoms and unsaturated double bonds contained in CDCA-Si-Pei combined with the iron atoms on the surface of the carbon steel to form a more stable complex, producing a thick film that blocks the erosion of HCl. The microscopic surface morphology results were consistent with the electrochemical test results;[40] it was also possible that the silicone shell formed by the organosilicon forms a good protection for the CDCA-Si-Pei surface.

The EDS distribution and Cl elemental content graphs were distributed in Fig. 9 when with the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei, the elements C, Fe, Cl and O were found by energy spectrum analysis, comparing the graphs (a) (e) and (i) revealed a significant decrease in the Cl elemental content and an increase in the Fe element, confirming that the corrosion of carbon steel was inhibited. By comparing the elemental scan analysis plots for Fe and Cl at different soaking times: (b, c) blank; (f, g) soaked 24 h; (j, k) soaked 144 h. At 24 h, the elemental and Cl content was the lowest, indicating that CDCA-Si-Pei had the most excellent inhibition at 24 h, which was consistent with the polarization curve and EIS analysis. By comparing (d), (h), and (l) it can be seen that the element Cl decreases after the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei, especially at 24 h, and was almost invisible. In contrast, the element Fe increases, which was since the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei inhibits the corrosion of carbon steel, so the element Cl decreases and, accordingly, the element Fe increases. At the same time, there was an increase in the element C, which indicated that the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei contains amide groups and unsaturated double bonds with the iron atoms on the surface of the carbon steel to form a more stable complex, generating a more dense film and that the Cl- in the solution can adsorb on the surface of the film, obviously blocking the attack and erosion of hydrochloric acid, which was consistent with the results observed in the SEM.

AFM was a powerful tool to evaluate the surface characteristics of the three-dimensional morphology of carbon steel surface. The corrosion test results of the surface morphology of carbon steel were shown in Fig. 10. Fig. 10 (a) showed the three-dimensional morphology of carbon steel soaked in a blank solution for 24 h and its unevenness, showing irregular morphology with an average roughness R
a was 161.0 nm. After adding CDCA-Si-Pei, comparing Fig. 10 (b) and (c), the surface of carbon steel became complete. The average roughness decreased significantly, with the R
a of soaking for 24 h and 144 h being 23.9 nm and 53.1 nm, both smaller than the R
a of the blank, indicating the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei effectively reduced the corrosion of the carbon steel. Meanwhile, the R
a of 24 h was smaller than that of 144 h, proving that the corrosion inhibition effect of 24 h was more excellent. Still, the overall was flatter, and the corrosion morphology analysis was consistent with the polarization and EIS analysis results.

The adsorption behavior of CDs on carbon steel surfaces in HCl environments followed the Langmuir adsorption model. Thus the Langmuir adsorption model can be used to evaluate the inhibition mechanism. The surface coverage (θ) can be calculated from Eq. (2): according to the Langmuir isothermal model, the equation Eq.(7) between θ and C:[41].
(7)
θ
1 − θ
= C
K
ads
Where: K
ads was the adsorption equilibrium constant. The adsorption free energy ΔGads through Eq.(8) was calculated using the adsorption equilibrium constant Kads:[42].
(8)
θ
1 − θ
K
ads
=
1
C
H 2 O
exp ⁡ （
− Δ G
ads
R
′
T
）
Through analysis, the R 2 values of CDCA-Si-Pei were 0.9999, illustrating that the adsorption behavior conformed to the Langmuir adsorption model. Meanwhile, the.

ΔG
ads was calculated from Eq.(8). Plotting C against C with C/θ, the resulting fit is shown in Fig. 11(a). It can be seen that there was an excellent linear relationship between C/θ and C. Through analysis, the R 2 values of CDCA-Si-Pei were 0.9999， illustrating that the adsorption behavior conformed to the Langmuir adsorption model.

Meanwhile, ΔG
ads was calculated from Eq.(8) as − 40.63 kJ/mol. The negative value of ΔG
ads indicated that the adsorption of CDCA-Si-Pei on the carbon steel surface was a spontaneous process, which facilitated the adsorption process of the protective film. Physical and chemical adsorption was typical between the corrosion inhibitor molecules and the carbon steel surface. When|ΔG
ads |< 20 kJ∙mol−1, the adsorption process was mainly physical adsorption; when|ΔG
ads |> 40 KJ∙mol−1, it was primarily chemical adsorption, and when|ΔG
ads | was between 20 kJ∙mol−1 and 440 kJ∙mol−1, it was both physical and chemical adsorption. CDCA-Si-Pei calculated ΔGads values between − 40 kJ/mol and − 20 kJ/mol, indicating that they were physically and chemically adsorbed on the Q235 carbon steel surface.

In order to quantitatively analyze the corrosion products, the Raman results were shown in Fig. 11(b). In the case of the Raman spectrum, two characteristic peaks at 154.27 cm−1, and 279.09 cm−1 were detected, which both were related to the Fe2O3; at the same time, 712.00 cm−1 was also detected corresponding toβ-FeOOH. Meanwhile, a number of weak peaks were also seen, which located at 356.00 cm−1 and 425.12 cm−1 were ascribed toγ-FeOOH and α-FeOOH separately, while 532.32 cm−1 and the 677.31 cm−1 both belonged to the Fe3O4 [43]. By contrast, the intensity of corrosion products on steel surface was greatly suppressed with the prolonged immersion time. Significantly, the peaks of Fe2O3 andβ-FeOOH almost disappeared when the steel was immersed in 24 h solution and 144 h, Which indicated CDCA-Si-Pei was an efficient corrosion inhibitor that dramatically inhibits the corrosion of carbon steel.

The corrosion and protection mechanisms of the carbon steel were presented in Fig. 12. From Fig. 12(a), it can be seen that without the addition of a corrosion inhibitor, the H+ in the HCl solution and the Fe on the surface of the carbon steel underwent a substitution reaction to form FeCl2 and H2 which escape from the surface of the carbon steel. This was due to the presence of lone pairs of electrons in the iron atom, which can form coordination bonds between the heteroatoms of CDCA-Si-Pei and the carbon steel substrate. After the addition of CDCA-Si-Pei, CDCA-Si-Pei was uniformly adsorbed on the steel surface to form a high strength bond, like a thick protective film to prevent the corrosion of carbon steel by reducing the exposure area of carbon steel in the HCl solution(Fig. 12(c)). HCl corrosion of carbon steel. It can be seen in Fig. 12 (b) that no H2 is produced, indicating that the corrosion of the carbon steel was inhibited and that CDCA-Si-Pei was an efficient and stable corrosion inhibitor.

The decision to use a corrosioninhibitor for a manufacturing process involves several considerations such as effectiveness, accessibility, and price efficiency. Furthermore, both human health and environmental concerns must be taken into account. As can be seen in Table 9, not only the CDCA-Si-Pei estimated optimum concentration was much lower than other inhibitors but the introduced inhibitor can guarantee 96.34 % inhibition efficiency, demonstrating promising protection performance compared to other inhibitors. Moreover, carbon dots were synthesized by selecting fewer toxic reagents with a fairly high corrosion inhibition efficiency at very low concentrations. the CDCA-Si-Pei was an efficient, green and environmentally friendly material (Table 9).

In summary， the synthesis of CDCA-Si-Pei was designed using a hydrothermal method, which exhibited good fluorescence properties and large functional groups. The relatively mild synthesis conditions of CDCA-Si-Pei allowed for better time and energy saving. Electrochemical analysis showed that CDCA-Si-Pei had a significant inhibitory effect on carbon steel, with an optimum dosing concentration of 10 mg/L compared to other CDs at 200 mg/L, achieving 96.34 % inhibition efficiency. In addition, the stability of the material was greatly improved, with CDCA-Si-Pei still having a high inhibition efficiency (94.79 %) after three months, and the immersion time (144 h) was approximately three times that of the available CDs. Calculations showed that the adsorption process of CDCA-Si-Pei on the carbon steel surface was under the Langmuir adsorption model and that the adsorption types were physical and chemical adsorption， demonstrating CDCA-Si-Pei was a multifunctional highly-efficient inhibitor.

Mi Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Ping He: Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hongjiang Zhang: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Sheying Dong: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Tinglin Huang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work performed was appreciated the support from the Scientific and Technological Research and Development Projects in Shaanxi Province (No. 2020GY-306) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21575111).
